summary: This guest post argues that upcoming labor reports will overstate job growth due to how seasonal adjustments work. Seasonal adjustments are statistical techniques that account for predictable yearly patterns in economic data, but they rely on historical trends that COVID-19 has disrupted. Virtual schooling has weakened the usual summer employment drops in education and among parents (especially women) who normally leave the workforce during school breaks. Since seasonal adjustments will still correct for the historical pattern of summer job losses that won't actually occur this year, the adjusted figures will artificially inflate apparent job growth in May and June 2021.
key_points: Seasonal adjustments correct raw economic data based on historical patterns, but these corrections assume incoming data follows the same seasonal trends as past data
Summer typically reduces employment both directly (education workers on break) and indirectly (parents, especially women, leaving workforce for childcare)
COVID-era virtual learning has already weakened these seasonal patterns, as many education support jobs disappeared and parents stayed home year-round
The August-September 2020 data confirms this effect: seasonally adjusted figures showed declines because the expected school-year job surge didn't materialize
The same logic means May-June 2021 reports will show inflated job growth because the expected summer job losses won't fully occur
money_quotes: The idea of seasonally adjusting data is that the meaning of a data point can depend on the time of year.
Seasonal adjustments to incoming data will be based on rigorous analysis of historical data and will implicitly presume incoming data will display the same seasonal trends as past data.
I'd argue that the disruption Covid has made to schooling is going to disrupt seasonal trends in employment.
With weaker seasonal effects now, seasonal adjustments which are based on historical data will over-account for them.
This misleading seasonally adjusted data will be what the media reports in terms of the unemployment rate and monthly job creation.
key_insight: Statistical methods that work well under normal conditions can produce misleading results when unprecedented disruptions like COVID-19 break the historical patterns those methods depend on.
